title: Discrete-Time Hazard Model For Time-To-Pit
summary: This target is naturally a next-event problem, not just a rowwise binary classification problem.
rationale: This target is naturally a next-event problem, not just a rowwise binary classification problem. Survival/AFT literature and recent F1 tyre-degradation work both model latent stint state with structural resets at pit stops, which matches the competition setting better than adding more static thresholds.
implementation_hint: Create a secondary training view per driver-race-stint where the label is hazard at lap t: pit on t+1 given no pit yet. Fit a tree model on stint-local features plus cumulative state, and optionally a second model for remaining-laps-to-pit using pseudo-survival labels within each observed stint. Blend hazard output back into the base classifier rather than replacing it outright.
expected_effect: Should improve ordering near true pit windows and reduce noisy positives on old tyres that are still strategically viable, especially late in stints.
risk: If CV is not strictly grouped by race-year or if stint-derived labels leak future information, local AUC can look falsely strong. Survival reformulation also adds engineering complexity.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
